title: What makes a feature good? Predictive utility, stability, availability, and cost
scope: Quality is broader than importance: availability at inference, stability over time/populations, sensitivity to measurement error, compute/storage/latency, interpretability, maintainability, robustness under shift. Ablation/SHAP become tools within this frame, not its definition.
key_concepts: Predictive utility (accuracy lift) is only one dimension of feature quality.
Availability at inference time determines if a feature can actually be used in production.
Stability ensures the feature distribution does not drift wildly over time.
Cost covers computation latency, storage requirements, and engineering maintenance.
definitions: Inference availability: The condition where the raw data needed to compute a feature is accessible at the exact moment a prediction is required.
Feature drift: The phenomenon where a feature's statistical properties change over time, degrading model performance.
examples: A feature requiring a 10-table join is highly predictive in training but has a 2-second latency, making it useless for real-time fraud detection.
relations_to_other_chapters: Sets the framing for Ch 9, moving beyond just ablation (Ch 9 s03) and SHAP (Ch 9 s05).
Points forward to Ch 17 regarding serving constraints.
